name: The Sadducees and the resurrection
notes: All three Synoptics record the Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, coming to Jesus with a riddle built on Moses' levirate law: seven brethren who in turn married one woman and died childless, then the woman herself. Their question is whose wife she shall be in the resurrection. Jesus answers that they err, not knowing the scriptures nor the power of God, for in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage but are as the angels, and He proves the resurrection from Moses at the bush, where God is the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the God not of the dead but of the living. Matthew and Mark phrase the rebuke 'Ye do err, not knowing the scriptures, nor the power of God,' while Luke expands the angelic vantage, adding that they are the children of God and of the resurrection and can die no more, and these unique words read in place. Each account closes from its own vantage: Matthew with the multitude astonished at His doctrine, Luke with certain scribes saying 'Master, thou hast well said'; here only the shared core is linked.
